coredata.dc:title: Composite of magnetic carbon quantum dot-supported ionic liquid and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) MOF: A triple catalytic composite for chemical transformations
coredata.dc:creator: Sadjadi, Samahe
Koohestani, Fatemeh
coredata.dc:description: A novel magnetic triple composite was synthesized through immobilization of DABCO-based ionic liquid on magnetic carbon quantum dot, followed by conjugation of the resultant compound with a copper-based metal-organic framework (CCDC no. 687690). The as-prepared composite was characterized via FESEM/EDS, XRD, TGA, FTIR, VSM and elemental mapping analysis and utilized as a magnetic catalyst for promoting three-component reaction of malononitrile, dimedone and aldehydes under mild reaction condition to furnish etrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans. The catalyst exhibited high catalytic activity and could promote the reaction of various aldehydes with different electronic densities. Moreover, the catalyst could be magnetically collected and successfully reused for five reaction runs with slight loss of the catalytic activity. Notably, comparison of the activity of the catalyst with that of ionic liquid, magnetic carbon quantum dot and metal-organic framework confirmed superior catalytic activity of the former.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Carbon quantum dot
Ionic liquid
Catalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Examining the catalytic activity: synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans
Effect of hybridization of the catalyst components on the catalytic activity
Comparison of the activity of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL with some reported catalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthetic procedure of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL.
TEM images of MNCQD (A) and MNCQD-IL (B).
FE-SEM images of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) (A) and the catalyst (B and C).
EDS and elemental mapping analyses of the catalyst.
VSM analyses of MNCQD, MNCQD-IL and the catalyst.
XRD patterns of MNCQD-IL, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) (inset) and the catalyst.
FTIR spectra of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), IL, MNCQD, MNCQD-IL and the catalyst.
TG curves of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), MNCQD, MNCQD-IL and the catalyst.
The plausible reaction mechanism for the synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans.
The results of recyclability test of the catalyst for the model reaction under optimum reaction condition.
Comparison of FTIR spectra of fresh and reused catalyst after six runs of the model reaction.
Study of the generality of the reaction a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Malononitrile (1 ​mmol), dimedone (1 ​mmol), aldehyde (1 ​mmol) and catalyst (30 ​mg) were dissolved in EtOH (15 ​mL) and mixed at 50 ​°C for 1 ​h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the activity of the as-prepared composite with some control catalysts for promoting the model reaction under optimum condition.
Comparison of the activity of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL for the synthesis of the model tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyran with other catalysts.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: H3PMo12O4 nanoparticles immobilized on ionic liquid decorated magnetic particles.

head.ce:title: Composite of magnetic carbon quantum dot-supported ionic liquid and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) MOF: A triple catalytic composite for chemical transformations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gas Conversion Department, Faculty of Petrochemicals, Iran Polymer and Petrochemical Institute, PO Box 14975-112, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gas Conversion Department, Faculty of Petrochemicals, Iran Polymer and Petrochemical Institute, PO Box 14975-112, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel magnetic triple composite was synthesized through immobilization of DABCO-based ionic liquid on magnetic carbon quantum dot, followed by conjugation of the resultant compound with a copper-based metal-organic framework (CCDC no. 687690). The as-prepared composite was characterized via FESEM/EDS, XRD, TGA, FTIR, VSM and elemental mapping analysis and utilized as a magnetic catalyst for promoting three-component reaction of malononitrile, dimedone and aldehydes under mild reaction condition to furnish etrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans. The catalyst exhibited high catalytic activity and could promote the reaction of various aldehydes with different electronic densities. Moreover, the catalyst could be magnetically collected and successfully reused for five reaction runs with slight loss of the catalytic activity. Notably, comparison of the activity of the catalyst with that of ionic liquid, magnetic carbon quantum dot and metal-organic framework confirmed superior catalytic activity of the former.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of ionic liquids, ILs, which are organic salts with organic cations and organic or inorganic anions [1–3], they have been extensively used for vast range of applications, such as catalysis, capacitors, batteries, sensors etc. These salts benefit from low melting points [1,4,5] and high conductivity and are considered as appealing solvents and catalysts for organic synthesis. To date, various chemical transformations have been promoted via ILs [6,7]. Furthermore, outstanding advances in the field of synthesis of ILs and development of various ILs types, such as basic ILs, amphiphilic ILs, acidic ILs and chiral ILs expanded their applications [8–12]. One of the drawbacks of ILs is their solubility in conventional solvents that makes their recovery and reuse tedious. As a solution to this issue, ILs can be stabilized on supporting materials to furnish heterogeneous ILs. Carbon compounds are one of the mostly studied supports for the stabilization of ILs [13]. In this regard, use of carbon nanomaterials has witnessed growing attraction [14]. One of the emerging class of carbon nanomaterials is carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which possess unique features, such as low toxicity, chemical stability and fine particle size [15,16]. These compounds can be simply prepared from low-cost and available raw materials, such as bio-wastes and carbohydrates [17–20]. The conventional methods for the preparation of CQDs include hydrothermal treatment, electrochemical etching, pyrolysis, laser ablation, microwave and ultrasonic-assisted approaches [21–23]. It is worth noting that CQDs not only can be used as a catalyst support, but also exhibit (photo) catalytic activity [20,24–29]. Moreover, CQDs can be readily magnetized to form magnetic catalysts with facile recovery [30]. In recent years, efficient catalytic systems have been reported by combining the properties of ILs and CQDs [31].

To further improve the catalytic performance of IL-based catalysts, they can be used in combination with other catalytic components. In this regard, use of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [32–34] has gained considerable attention. MOFs are porous nanomaterials that are composed of organic linkers and metallic nodes. These organic/inorganic hybrids can be synthesized through known synthetic approaches, such as hydrothermal treatment, self-assembly [35–37] and novel methods, such as surfactant-thermal method [38–41]. The porosity of MOF can be adjusted by varying the type of the used linkers. This issue allows to design task-specific MOFs for specific applications [42–45]. One of the well-known MOF is copper-based MOF. As copper is low-cost, abundant and non-toxic, it has been extensively used for the preparation of MOFs, such as Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) that is prepared from copper nitrate and commercially-available and low-toxic terephthalic acid (BDC) [46]. In fact, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) show attractive properties, such as high stability in aqueous media at different pHs. This issue can be attributed to the strong coordination interaction between copper cations and –COOH group of BDC.

To date, various composites of ILs and MOFs have been developed for the catalytic purposes [47,48]. Mostly, combination of these two components led to improvement of the performance of the resultant composite.

Considering the above-mentioned issues and in attempt to develop an IL-based catalyst with high activity and recyclability, we report a novel magnetic composite composed of magnetic CQD (MNCQD), IL and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and study its catalytic performance for promoting synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans that is a class of biologically active heterocycles [48–50] under mild reaction condition.

The reagents utilized for the synthesis of the catalytic composite and evaluation of its catalytic performance included: BDC, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, (3-chloropropyl) trimethoxysilane (CPTMS), citric acid, diazabicyclo [2.2.2]octane (DABCO), ethylenediamine, iron(III) chloride hexahydrate, iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate, ammonia, aldehyde, dimedone, malononitrile, N,N -dimethylformamide (DMF), toluene, diethylether, EtOH and MeOH, all was provided from Sigma-Aldrich.

To confirm the formation of the composite, the following analyses have been applied: Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP-AES, via Varian, Vista-pro instrument), Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM, using Lakeshore 7407, at room temperature), Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR, using PERKIN-ELMER-Spectrum 65 with KBr pellet), X-ray Diffraction (XRD, using Siemens, D5000 apparatus with graphite monochromatic Cu-Kα), Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA, via METTLER TOLEDO instrument under N2 atmosphere at heating rate of 10 ​°C.min−1), elemental mapping analysis, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) as well as Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) via MIRA 3 TESCAN-XMU, Brunauer, Emmett and Teller (BET, using BELSORP Mini II instrument). The pre-heating condition for BET analysis was heating at 423 ​K for 3 ​h.

In the first step, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) was prepared through previously reported method [49]. Briefly, a solution of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (1 ​mmol) and BDC (1 ​mmol) in DMF (40 ​mL) was hydrothermally treated at 120 ​°C for 15 ​h. Subsequently, the autoclave was cooled down slowly and the mixture was centrifuged (rate ​= ​5000 rmp). To purify the obtained Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), it was washed repeatedly with DMF and then dried at ambient temperature overnight.

To prepare MNCQD, CQD was first prepared through a known method [50]. Briefly, a solution of citric acid (0.5 ​g in H2O (100 ​mL)) and ethylenediamine (5.0 ​mL) was hydrothermally treated at 200 ​°C for 5 ​h. The reaction mixture was then centrifuged to obtain CQD.

To prepare MNPs, two iron salts, FeCl3·6H2O (1.37 ​g) and FeCl2·4H2O (0.5 ​g) were dissolved in deionized water (10 ​mL) and stirred for 2 ​h. Afterwards, NH3 (10 ​mL) was slowly added to the iron solution and the resultant solution was refluxed at 60 ​°C for 1 ​h. Then, the precipitate was magnetically collected and washed with deionized water and dried at ambient temperature overnight. In next step, the as-prepared CQD (20 ​mL) and MNPs (1 ​g) were mixed in EtOH under ultrasonic condition (power 100 ​W for 15 ​min) and then refluxed for 2 ​h at 85 ​°C under Ar atmosphere. Finally, the solid was collected magnetically and dried at room temperature overnight.

To decorate the as-prepared MNCQD with IL, IL was first synthesized through refluxing of DABCO (10 ​mmol) and CPTMS (10 ​mmol) in toluene (50 ​mL) for 48 ​h under Ar atmosphere [51]. The resultant IL was washed with diethylether (3 ​× ​10 ​mL) and dried overnight at ambient temperature. Subsequently, MNCQD (0.6 ​g) and IL (1.5 ​g) were mixed in MeOH (50 ​mL) and refluxed for 24 ​h. Finally, the precipitate was magnetically collected, washed with MeOH and dried at room temperature overnight.

In the final step, the composite of the as-prepared Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL was prepared by mixing of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) (1 ​g) and MNCQD-IL (0.5 ​g) in EtOH (10 ​mL), followed by sonication for 24 ​h at room temperature. It is worth noting that the used ultrasonic apparatus was equipped with a thermal sensor and control of the temperature was carried out by using cold water bath. Upon completion of the reaction, the precipitate was separated via centrifuge, washed with EtOH and dried at room temperature overnight, Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examining the catalytic activity: synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the catalytic performance of the as-prepared catalyst, synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans was conducted. To this purpose, malononitrile (1 ​mmol), dimedone (1 ​mmol) and aldehyde (1 ​mmol) were dissolved in EtOH (15 ​mL) and then the catalyst (30 ​mg) was added. The mixture was subsequently mixed at 50 ​°C for 1 ​h and the reaction progress was monitored using thin layer chromatography (TLC). At the end of the reaction, the catalyst was recovered via filtration and the organic product was achieved after solvent evaporation. Purification of the product was conducted via recrystallization and column chromatography. To reuse the recovered catalyst, it was washed with EtOH and dried in oven at 60 ​°C overnight.

In this study, not only characterization of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL was carried out, but also the as-prepared Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), MNCQD and MNCQD-IL were characterized and their features were compared with those of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) -MNCQD-IL.

Initially, the effect of conjugation of IL on the morphology of MNCQD was investigated. To this purpose, the TEM images of MNCQD and MNCQD-IL were recorded and compared, Fig. 2 . As shown, MNCQD showed spherical morphology and due to the magnetic feature of MNCQD, the spheres are close together, Fig. 2 A. This observation is in a good agreement with the literature [52]. Comparison of Fig. 2 A and B clearly indicates that introduction of IL remarkably affected the morphology. In more detail, in MNCQD-IL, Fig. 2 B, the spheres packed together to form aggregates. This issue can be attributed to the presence of IL. In fact, IL can induce aggregation through improving electrostatic interactions between particles.

To investigate the morphology of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL, it was subjected to FE-SEM analysis. Moreover, to elucidate the effect of incorporation of MNCQD-IL on the morphology of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), the FE-SEM image of the catalyst was compared with that of bare Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3 A, the as-synthesized Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) showed cubic morphology. In FE-SEM images of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL, the Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) cubes could be observed. This observation implied that Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) preserved its morphology in the course of hybridization with MNCQD-IL. Notably, precise comparison of the FE-SEM images of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL indicated that surface of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL is rougher compared to that of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and is covered with small semi-spherical particles with average particle size of 34.3 ​nm. Considering the TEM results of MNCQD-IL, these particles can be assigned to the MNCQD-IL aggregates.

EDS and elemental mapping analyses of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL were also carried out to verify conjugation of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL. As shown in Fig. 4 , EDS analysis of the composite confirms the presence of C, N, Cu, Si, Fe, Cl and O atoms in the structure of the as-prepared catalyst. In this analysis, Cu, O and C atoms can be attributed to Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) structure. Moreover, Fe, C and O can indicate formation of MNCQD, while Cl, N, Si, C and O atoms can be representative of IL. Elemental mapping analysis of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL is also depicted in Fig. 4. As shown, Fe, C, N, Cl, O and Si atoms exhibit homogeneous dispersion. This observation can approve that MNCQD-IL dispersed uniformly on Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690).

To elucidate the magnetic property of the as-synthesized MNCQD, it was analyzed via VSM analysis, Fig. 5 . The result confirmed that the magnetic saturation value (Ms) for MNCQD was 100 emu g−1. Upon introduction of IL, and synthesis of MNCQD-IL, Ms value significantly decreased and reached to 43.2 emu g−1. This result is due to the coverage of magnetic MNCQD with non-magnetic IL. In fact, decrement of Ms value can be a proof for the incorporation of IL and formation of MNCQD-IL. VSM analysis of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL showed that Ms value in this sample decreased to 8 emu g−1. Similarly, this observation can be ascribed to the incorporation of non-magnetic Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690). It is worth noting that despite of low Ms value, the sample was magnetic enough to be collected by using an external magnet.

In Fig. 6 the XRD patterns of MNCQD-IL, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) -MNCQD-IL are illustrated. As shown in Fig. 6 (inset), the characteristic peaks of the synthesized Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) are discerned at 2θ ​= ​9.9°, 12.0°, 13.5°, 17.1°, 20.4°, 24.9°, 34.1°, 42.2°. It is also clear that the characteristic peaks of MNCQD-IL appeared at 2θ ​= ​30.6°, 36.3°, 43.6°, 54.0°, 58.1°, 63.6° and 75.2° (JCPDS card no. 39–1346). Comparing the XRD pattern of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL with that of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL, it can be observed that the XRD pattern of the catalyst contains both characteristic peaks of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL. This issue not only approved conjugation of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) -MNCQD-IL, but also affirmed that both components preserved their structures in the course of hybridization.

To further approve formation of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), IL, MNCQD, MNCQD-IL and the catalyst, their FTIR spectrum was recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 7 , the absorbance bands of MNCQD appeared at 3427 ​cm−1 that is due to –OH groups, 2926 ​cm−1 that can be assigned to -C-H functionality, 1603 ​cm−1 that is representative of –CC bonds and 1015 ​cm−1 that can be attributed to -C-O functionality. In the FTIR spectrum of IL, the following characteristic bands can be observed: the band at 1101 ​cm−1 that is due to –Si-O, 2942 ​cm−1 that is indicative of –C-H bond, 1619 ​cm−1 that is assigned to –C-N bond and the band at 3451 ​cm−1 that is representative of –OH functionality. In the FTIR spectrum of MNCQD-IL, both characteristic bands of IL and MNCQD can be observed.

FTIR spectrum of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) showed the absorbance bands at 1579 ​cm−1 and 769 ​cm−1 that are due to the vibration of aromatic ring, the bands at 1376 ​cm−1 and 1454 ​cm−1 that are assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of –CO2H respectively, the band at 1654 ​cm−1 that is representative of –CO group and the band at 3444 ​cm−1 that is attributed to the –OH groups [53]. FTIR spectrum of the catalyst possesses the absorbance bands of both Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL, indicating conjugation of both components.

TG analysis was also exploited for confirming the formation of the intermediates and the final catalyst. In this regard, TG curves of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), MNCQD, MNCQD-IL and the catalyst were obtained and compared, Fig. 8 . In TG curve of MNCQD, two weight losses (one due to loss of water at 120 ​°C and another one at 430 ​°C due to the degradation of MNCQD) can be detected. In TG curve of MNCQD-IL, apart from the aforementioned weight losses, an additional one in the range of 250–330 ​°C can be observed that can be assigned to the degradation of IL. According to TGA results, the content of IL on MNCQD-IL is about 16 ​wt%. In the case of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), two weight losses at 100–210 ​°C (water loss) and 400–430 ​°C (decomposition) can be discerned. Finally, TG curve of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL showed three weight losses due to water loss (at temperature ​= ​150 ​°C), decomposition of MNCQD-IL (at temperature ​= ​330 ​°C) and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) decomposition (at temperature ​= ​430 ​°C). According to these results, the content of MNCQD-IL in the composite was about 25 ​wt%.

In the next section, it was elucidated whether the as-prepared composite is catalytically active. In this regard, the performance of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL for promoting a conventional organic transformation, i.e. synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans from three-component reaction of malononitrile, dimedone and aldehyde, was examined (Fig. 9 ).

First, optimization of the reaction variables was conducted. In this context, reaction of malononitrile, dimedone and benzaldehyde was selected as a model reaction and repeated in the presence of 0.03 ​g of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL in different solvents, including water, EtOH, water: EtOH (1:1) mixture and CH3CN at ambient temperature. Comparison of the yields of the reactions indicated that the nature of the reaction solvent could affect the yield of the reaction and the best reaction yield was achieved in EtOH. Next, the same reaction was carried out in EtOH in the presence of different dosages of the catalyst (0.01–0.04 ​g). The results established that the catalyst content is an important factor on the yield of the reaction and its optimum amount was 0.03 ​g. Use of lower catalyst loading (0.01–0.02 ​g) led to lower yields of the desired product, while increase of the catalyst amount to 0.04 ​g had no effect on the reaction. Finally, the reaction temperature was optimized by repeating the model reaction at different temperatures, ranging from 25 to 70 ​°C. The results indicated that increase of the reaction temperature to 50 ​°C improved the rate and the yield of the reaction. Notably, further increase in the reaction temperature was not effective. Hence, the optimum reaction condition was use of 0.03 ​g Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL in EtOH at 50 ​°C. Using the optimum reaction condition, the model product could be furnished in 97% yield.

To verify the generality of the present protocol, synthesis of various tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans was performed by employing different aldehydes with different electronic natures. The results are summarized in Table 1 . As tabulated, not only benzaldehyde could undergo the reaction under Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL catalysis to furnish high yields of the corresponding products, other aldehydes with both electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups could lead to high yields of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans. These results confirmed that this protocol can be generalized to other substrates.

The plausible reaction mechanism for the synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans under Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL catalysis is illustrated in Fig. 10 . As depicted, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL can activate the carbonyl functionality of aldehyde. The activated aldehyde, then, undergoes the reaction with malononitrile to furnish an intermediate, which subsequently reacts with dimedone. Finally, cyclization of the resulting intermediate gives the desired product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of hybridization of the catalyst components on the catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, the catalytic studies affirmed high activity of the as-prepared catalytic composite. Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL is composed of three main components, including IL, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD. The reason for the incorporation of IL in the structure of the composite is its catalytic nature [54]. MNCQD on the other hand, can play the role of support for IL and heterogenize IL. Moreover, the magnetic nature of MNCQD can effectively facilitate the recovery and recycling of the catalyst. The reports on the Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) composites implied that incorporation of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) can improve the performance of the composite [55]. In fact, the metal ions-clusters in the framework of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) coordinate to the carbonyl group of the aldehyde and activates it [56]. Hence, in this study, this triple composite was devised. To investigate the role of each component in the catalysis, the catalytic activity of some control samples, including, IL, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL for promoting the model reaction was examined and compared with that of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL, Table 2 .

As listed in Table 2, IL showed high catalytic activity and led to the formation of the desired product in 80% yield. However, the drawback of IL was its tedious recovery from the reaction medium. Regarding MNCQD, low catalytic activity was observed that can originate from participation of the functional groups of MNCQD in the catalysis. In fact, the main role of MNCQD is stabilization of IL. The results showed that the catalytic activity of MNCQD-IL was superior to IL and MNCQD. This issue can be ascribed to the synergistic effects between IL and MNCQD. As shown, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) in its individual form was an inefficient catalyst for the model reaction. However, its conjugation with MNCQD-IL resulted in improvement of the catalytic activity. As mentioned earlier, this issue can be attributed to the role of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) in the activation of the aldehyde and possible synergism between Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL. These results indicate the beneficiary effect of hybridization of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and MNCQD-IL on the catalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the activity of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL with some reported catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Although the focus of this research was devising a novel catalytic composite, and synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyran was just used to disclose the catalytic activity of the as-prepared composite, the activity of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL for the synthesis of the model tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyran was compared with some of the previously reported catalysts, Table 3 , to elucidate whether Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL can be considered as an efficient catalyst for this reaction. As show in Table 3, this reaction has been promoted via various catalysts. Moreover, catalyst-free procedures have also been reported. As shown in Entry 6, synthesis of the model reaction in the absence of the catalyst was very time consuming. Other tabulated methodologies have been mostly conducted in aqueous media. The comparison of the yields of the model product confirms that the activity of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL is comparable with the reported procedures. Noteworthy, compared to the reported homogeneous catalysts, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL benefits from facile recovery.

To evaluate the recyclability of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL, the model reaction was repeated in the presence of the recovered Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL under similar reaction condition. As depicted in Fig. 11 , the recovered catalyst could be successfully reused with no significant decrement of its activity. As shown, only slight decrement of the catalytic activity was observed upon recycling and the desired product was achieved in 87% yield in the sixth run of the reaction.

In Fig. 12 , the comparison of FTIR spectra of fresh and reused Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) -MNCQD-IL (after sixth run) is presented. As shown, in two spectra the characteristic bands of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL are observable. However, there are some differences between the two spectra that can be due to the consecutive washing of the catalyst in the course of recovery and reuse and possible deposition of the substrates and products on the surface of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL.

To check the possibility of leaching of MOF, the reused catalyst after 6 runs was characterized via ICP. The results showed that the content of copper was slightly lower than that of the fresh catalyst (2 ​wt% lower than initial content). Considering these results, decrement of the catalytic activity was attributed to the slight leaching of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690) and possibly, coverage of catalytic sites with the organic reagents and products.

A triple composite, consist of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690), IL and MNCQD is designed and fabricated through stabilization of IL on the as-prepared MNCQD, followed by conjugation of the resulting compound with Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690). The obtained composite, Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL, was then used as a catalyst for promoting one-pot three component synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans. The catalytic studies approved high catalytic activity of the catalyst under mild reaction condition. On the other hand, the catalyst could be magnetically recovered and reused for at least six consecutive reaction runs with almost slight loss of its catalytic activity. A comparative study also affirmed that the activity of Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690)-MNCQD-IL was superior compared to IL, MNCQD, MNCQD-IL and Cu-BDC (CCDC no. 687690).

Samahe Sadjadi: Conceptualization; Funding acquisition; Project administration; Resources; Supervision; Writing - review & editing. Fatemeh Koohestani: Formal analysis; Data curation; Methodology; Visualization; Investigation; Writing - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the partial support of Iran Polymer and Petrochemical Institute.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-Z. Lian, Y. Huang, Y.-Q. Li, W.-J. Zheng, A Green Synthesis of Tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyran Derivatives through Three-Component Condensation Using N-Methylimidazole as Organocatalyst, Monatsh. Chem. 139(2) (2008) 129-131.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile and efficient synthesis of 2-amino-3-cyano-4H-chromenes and tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans using 2,2,2-trifluoroethanol as a metal-free and reusable medium
J. Fluor. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Khaksar, A. Rouhollahpour, S.M. Talesh, A facile and efficient synthesis of 2-amino-3-cyano-4H-chromenes and tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans using 2,2,2-trifluoroethanol as a metal-free and reusable medium, J. Fluor. Chem. 141 (2012) 11-15.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A recyclable and highly effective ZnO-beta zeolite as a catalyst for one-pot three-component synthesis of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans, chin
J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Katkar, M.K. Lande, B.R. Arbad, S.T. Gaikwad, A Recyclable and Highly Effective ZnO-beta Zeolite as a Catalyst for One-pot Three-Component Synthesis of Tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans, Chin. J. Chem. 29(1) (2011) 199-202.
